title: Perfection and Christ's Blessing
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This short reflection was offered by Rob some weeks before he died. While someone might choose to listen to this as a ‘stand alone’ offering, it really belongs in and forms a part of the ‘Soulmaking Dharma’ body of teachings and will best make soulful sense to a listener who has some practice understanding of the foundations of a Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: In this short reflection, Rob Burbea rethinks perfection as something present within being itself, rather than as achievement or completion. Using the small act of washing hands while weakened and ill, he points toward a beauty or grace that can be sensed in ordinary contact, loss, and even dukkha. He then turns to Christ’s blessing as a contemplative lens for this same kind of seeing, before briefly naming the problem of theodicy and returning to the heart’s quieter knowing.
short_summary: Rob Burbea reflects on perfection as a grace that can be sensed in weakness, loss, and dukkha itself.

chapters.title: A different perfection
chapters.description: Rob opens by contrasting ordinary ideas of perfection with a deeper perfection that runs through existence.

chapters.title: Hands, weakness, grace
chapters.description: He uses the image of washing his hands while physically diminished to point to a perfection and beauty present in simple touch.

chapters.title: Seeing, not achieving
chapters.description: Perfection is framed as a way of seeing that is at once universal and personal, woven with love and being known.

chapters.title: Christ’s blessing
chapters.description: Rob introduces Christ’s blessing as something that seems to rest on everything, especially in the experience of loss, weakness, and incapacity.

chapters.title: Beyond theodicy
chapters.description: He briefly names theodicy, then moves away from explanation toward the soul’s capacity to sense and taste something direct.

chapters.title: Blessing in dukkha
chapters.description: The talk closes by returning to the claim that Christ’s blessing is on everything, including the palpable dukkha itself.

title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The five talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. Being preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, the exercises offered in these talks can be worked with before a student engages the full scope of the Soulmaking teachings. However, some of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended and contextualised once a student already has some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of A Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a short preliminary practice that combines voicing, posture, and small movement as a way of training sensitivity. He demonstrates how to vary a simple sustained sound across gesture, pitch, loudness, and bodily orientation, then invites listeners to notice effects in the energy body, emotions, and soul resonance. The talk stays practical and exploratory, emphasizing repetition, subtlety, and not expecting too much from the exercise.
short_summary: A brief guided exploration of how voice and gesture can be combined to refine sensitivity to body, emotion, and soul resonance.

chapters.title: Setting Up the Exercise
chapters.description: Rob frames the practice as a modest exercise and situates it among earlier movement-based preliminaries.

chapters.title: Image, Voice, Movement
chapters.description: He describes two directions for the practice: letting image move into voice and posture, or starting with movement and noticing what it does in the soul.

chapters.title: Voicing a Simple Sound
chapters.description: He introduces the combined sound-and-gesture exercise and demonstrates a sustained syllable with rough rhythmic repetition.

chapters.title: Open and Crossed Postures
chapters.description: The talk turns to how different arm positions and slight torso rotation change the felt quality of the same voiced sound.

chapters.title: Varying the Gesture
chapters.description: Rob explores crossed hands, folded arms, and other postures while encouraging slow repetition and close attention.

chapters.title: Pitch and Loudness
chapters.description: He adds further variables, including register and volume, from loud voice to whisper and even internal sounding.

chapters.title: Noticing the Effects
chapters.description: The practice is summed up as a study in sensitivity, with attention to energy body, emotion, image, and soul resonance.

title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The five talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. Being preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, the exercises offered in these talks can be worked with before a student engages the full scope of the Soulmaking teachings. However, some of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended and contextualised once a student already has some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of A Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits exercise four from a series on voice, movement, and gesture, and gently shifts it away from performing an idea of blessing toward sensing blessing in the moment. He then widens the frame through mantra, the felt mystery of sound, and the role of self-consciousness, metta, and compassion in dyadic practice. The second half turns to iconic images and moments — Christ on the Via Dolorosa, the Buddha touching the earth, Rosa Parks refusing to move, and Abraham welcoming the angels — as ways of entering bodily, ethical, and imaginal sensitivity.
short_summary: A quiet refinement of movement-and-voice practice that moves from blessing and mantra into imaginal examples of courage, receptivity, and embodied soulmaking.

chapters.title: Blessing as lived intention
chapters.description: Rob returns to exercise four and invites listeners to begin from the intention to bless, rather than from a pre-planned idea of what blessing should look like.

chapters.title: Voice beyond meaning
chapters.description: He turns to mantra and the sound of speech itself, showing how vocalization can carry meaning while also opening a dimension of mystery beyond meaning.

chapters.title: Relational sensitivity
chapters.description: The talk centers on the witness role, subtle effects in the energy body, and practical responses to self-consciousness, fear of judgment, and blockage.

chapters.title: Imaginal dyad variations
chapters.description: Rob introduces the exercise with an actual partner or an imaginal figure, and explains why each form may support different kinds of subtlety and openness.

chapters.title: Christ and the earth
chapters.description: He uses Christ on the way to execution and the Buddha's earth-touching gesture as iconic moments that can be sensed from within rather than merely acted out.

chapters.title: Abraham's hospitality
chapters.description: The focus shifts to the soul-making poise of waiting, receptivity, and hospitality to angels before an image fully appears.

chapters.title: Rosa Parks' immovability
chapters.description: Rosa Parks becomes the central example of bodily courage, dignity, and ethical clarity, with Emmett Till and the bus boycott deepening the image.

chapters.title: Tantric language and Buddha nature
chapters.description: Rob briefly turns to a Sanskrit tantric formula and the Buddha's flexible self-designation as Tathagata to show how language can remain layered and alive.

chapters.title: Preliminaries that deepen
chapters.description: He closes by reflecting on preliminaries in Tibetan practice and on how foundational exercises can expand into something far from merely basic.

title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The five talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. Being preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, the exercises offered in these talks can be worked with before a student engages the full scope of the Soulmaking teachings. However, some of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended and contextualised once a student already has some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of A Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins by using a personal period of being overwhelmed to question the ordinary idea of “centering,” then opens that term into a wider critique of selfhood shaped by interiority, boundaries, and singularity. From there he contrasts apophatic emptiness with an imaginal sense of angel, soul, image, and participation, and shows how those terms can change how difficulty, practice, and relationship are understood. The talk closes by introducing a dyad exercise in blessing through movement, gesture, and voice, first with meaning and then with a more stripped-down mode that lets expression move beyond ordinary language.
short_summary: A reflection on centering and imaginal personhood that ends with a detailed dyad practice of blessing through movement, gesture, and voice.

chapters.title: Opening and orientation
chapters.description: Rob previews the exercise to come and spends time clarifying the vocabulary he wants to examine first.

chapters.title: What centering implies
chapters.description: He uses a recent period of pressure and disorientation to question centering as a model of self, boundary, and inner core.

chapters.title: Emptiness and imaginal presence
chapters.description: He contrasts Christian centering prayer and apophatic emptiness with the angelic or imaginal mode he is emphasizing.

chapters.title: Stretch, devotion, and risk
chapters.description: He links soul, reverence, humility, eros, and antinomy to the conditions under which imaginal presence may arise.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and the person
chapters.description: He compares soulmaking language with aggregate-based analysis, arguing that soul, image, and angel keep personhood and relationality in view.

chapters.title: Meaning, participation, and image
chapters.description: He deepens the discussion of angelic and imaginal language as unfathomable, participatory, and not simply one or two.

chapters.title: First blessing exercise
chapters.description: He introduces the dyad practice in which one person blesses and the other receives through movement, gesture, and voice without known language.

chapters.title: Beyond ordinary meaning
chapters.description: He offers a second mode of the same exercise that drops any attempt to convey meaning, story, or emotion, and lets the practice move beyond blessing in the usual sense.

title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The five talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. Being preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, the exercises offered in these talks can be worked with before a student engages the full scope of the Soulmaking teachings. However, some of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended and contextualised once a student already has some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of A Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a talk on preliminary Soulmaking practices, widening the frame from earlier exercises into why these small practices matter. He connects movement, gesture, and voice to sensitivity, blocked energy, and the possibility that soul can express itself more fully in practice and ordinary life. Later he introduces five versions of a third exercise and keeps returning to the question of discernment: when to concretize, when to stay with image, and what helps practice become sustainable over time.
short_summary: A careful explanation of why movement, gesture, and voice practices matter for Soulmaking Dharma, with five new exercises and a strong emphasis on discernment and long-term consequences.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: He situates this talk as a continuation of earlier material and asks whether listeners have actually worked with the previous exercises.

chapters.title: Why the preliminaries matter
chapters.description: He explains how these small practices reveal inertia, discernment, and the difference between merely hearing teachings and taking them up.

chapters.title: Sensitivity and holding back
chapters.description: He develops two main reasons for the practices: increasing sensitivity and noticing how body, voice, and energy are habitually held in.

chapters.title: Regret and the middle way
chapters.description: Using his own regret about jazz technique, he argues that these exercises are offered so people do not later regret not supporting what matters most.

chapters.title: Exercise three begins
chapters.description: He introduces the third exercise series and first names the 'thunderclap' practice, a brief emphatic movement that gathers intention, body, and soul.

chapters.title: Angels and the raging beast
chapters.description: He presents two more modes of practice: being borne aloft by angels, and allowing a raging beast to move through the body and voice.

chapters.title: Guardian and serpent
chapters.description: He adds the immovable protector and the serpent, keeping the names open-ended and emphasizing that each practice can be done physically or imaginally.

chapters.title: Circuits and consequences
chapters.description: He explains why these 'technical' exercises matter: they can reopen blocked circuits that limit imagination, emotion, and life choices.

chapters.title: Discernment in life
chapters.description: He closes by linking the exercises to being seen, judged, and called into life, including examples from Goenka retreats, the Ridwan school, and imaginal help from angels and demons.

title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The five talks in this series were recorded by Rob at his home. Being preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, the exercises offered in these talks can be worked with before a student engages the full scope of the Soulmaking teachings. However, some of the material presented here will only be properly comprehended and contextualised once a student already has some basis of preparatory experience and understanding of A Soulmaking Dharma.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces a set of five preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, explaining why he is offering them now and why they center on movement, gesture, and voice. Before the first exercise, he lingers on the larger question of how to listen: not only for specific instructions, but for the deeper conceptual structure that gives them meaning. The talk keeps returning to the risk of narrowing practice into a small goal, and instead points toward sensitivity, development, and a wider sense of what practice can serve.
short_summary: Rob Burbea introduces preliminary movement and voice practices for Soulmaking Dharma, while arguing for more attentive listening and a broader, less narrow understanding of practice.

chapters.title: Opening the preliminaries
chapters.description: He explains why he is recording these talks now and frames them as practical preliminaries rather than a finished presentation.

chapters.title: Movement, voice, and development
chapters.description: He describes the exercises as centered on movement, gesture, and voice, and emphasizes that they should be developed carefully and organically.

chapters.title: Listening for instructions
chapters.description: He invites listeners to go back through earlier talks and extract hidden instructions and larger principles, rather than passively receiving teachings.

chapters.title: Why this focus matters
chapters.description: He connects movement, gesture, and voice to the capacities needed to 'fly' in practice, and to the possibility of hearing and reading more structurally.

chapters.title: Inertia and structure
chapters.description: He reflects on habits of listening and practice, the rarity of structural listening, and the value of seeing a whole conceptual framework before assessing details.

chapters.title: Desire and the path
chapters.description: Using jhana practice and then a retreat anecdote, he argues that desire, goals, and the fantasy of practice matter more deeply than isolated techniques.

chapters.title: What practice is for
chapters.description: He broadens the critique of narrow Dharma goals, brings in Einstein and Beethoven as contrasts to premature completion, and turns to the Four Noble Truths and the question of suffering.

chapters.title: Exercise one: movement
chapters.description: He introduces the first exercise, a movement practice that can be done physically or in imagination, with attention to posture, expansion, and the sensing of space.

chapters.title: Exercise two: voice
chapters.description: He outlines the second exercise, using extended vocal tones to explore resonance, whole-body emanation, mixed awareness, and sound filling the space.

title: Practising with Death and Dying
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores death and dying through the lens of Soulmaking Dharma, moving away from familiar Buddhist and secular views toward a more phenomenological inquiry. After briefly setting aside impermanence, afterlife, rebirth, and even physics-based analogies, he turns to practice-based ways of seeing that can make death feel less flat and less final. The talk then opens into gifts, duty, beauty, grief, and imaginal relationship, including extended examples of a friend’s terminal illness and how death itself can become a beloved presence.
short_summary: A Soulmaking Dharma exploration of death as something that can be met through practice, imaginal relationship, and a fuller sense of personhood rather than through fixed doctrines.

chapters.title: Opening the Question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces death and dying as a topic and begins by naming the common cultural approaches that usually shape the conversation.

chapters.title: Views To Set Aside
chapters.description: He surveys impermanence, annihilationism, eternalism, and rebirth, showing how each can become a limiting frame for death.

chapters.title: Physics And Perception
chapters.description: A detour through quantum entanglement and observation leads into the larger point that inherited assumptions shape what appears real.

chapters.title: Phenomenology Of Death
chapters.description: He turns decisively toward Soulmaking Dharma, emphasizing ways of looking, mixed cultural inheritance, and what can make death feel more open than mere resignation.

chapters.title: What Helps Death
chapters.description: Burbea considers ordinary strategies like achievement, longevity, and impermanence practice, then contrasts them with a deeper opening through emptiness and direct knowing.

chapters.title: Gift, Duty, Beauty
chapters.description: He develops four main supports: life as gift, trying to do one’s duty, living toward the worthwhile, and receiving the beauty of a life already shaped by these currents.

chapters.title: Burying The Beloved
chapters.description: Using John Coltrane, Bach, and Clement of Alexandria, he explores grief, providence, and the possibility that even tragic death can be held as gift and beauty.

chapters.title: Image And Relationship
chapters.description: Burbea builds a detailed account of image, analogue, and soul-making relationship, arguing that personhood is already multi-stranded and not exhausted by physical life.

chapters.title: A Death Re-Enchanted
chapters.description: He reads a friend’s written reflections on metastatic cancer and a black snake dream as examples of death becoming imaginal, beloved, and even eternal in a non-literal sense.

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 6 - An Inconclusive Conclusion, or A Prolegomenon)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea closes this part of the Image of Ethics series with a deliberately open-ended reflection on ethics, precepts, and Soulmaking Dharma. He argues that the five precepts are valuable but incomplete, and then widens the frame through climate change, power, patriarchy, ceremony, and the question of whether teaching should be freely available. The talk moves from caution about misunderstanding to a broader inquiry into values, hierarchies of value, and the safeguards needed when imaginal and emptiness teachings are taken up.
short_summary: An open-ended closing reflection on why ethics needs more than precepts, and how Soulmaking Dharma, value, and readiness change the way the questions are asked.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: He frames the talk as a partial, provisional continuation of earlier ethics and soulmaking material rather than a final conclusion.

chapters.title: Why precepts are not enough
chapters.description: He argues that every ethical system is incomplete, including the Buddhist precepts and even Soulmaking Dharma as an ethical approach.

chapters.title: Complacency and shadow
chapters.description: He looks at the dangers of easy confidence in the precepts, including different interpretations, power abuse, and passive aggression.

chapters.title: From rules to values
chapters.description: He distinguishes precepts from values and asks why Buddhism was so slow to respond to climate change and other ethical crises.

chapters.title: Ceremony and safety
chapters.description: He considers whether retreat openings should include a precepts ceremony, while insisting that safety alone is still not enough.

chapters.title: Prerequisites and readiness
chapters.description: He turns to the order of practice, asking how much emptiness understanding people need before Soulmaking Dharma, and whether the teachings should be publicly accessible.

chapters.title: Hartmann and hierarchy
chapters.description: Using Hartmann, Augustine, and examples like altruism and egoism, he develops the idea that ethics starts with what is of value, not only what is right or wrong.

chapters.title: Nobility and the remote
chapters.description: He explores historical shifts in values through Christianity and warns against over-prioritizing distant or lofty ideals at the expense of ordinary human care.

chapters.title: Imaginal safeguards
chapters.description: He returns to imaginal practice, distinguishing eros from craving, outlining safeguards in the middle way, and revisiting the question of who should receive these teachings and when.

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his inquiry into how Soulmaking Dharma reshapes ethics by asking what kind of human being ethics presupposes and creates. He moves from lived experience, including the contrast between soul-level and bodily suffering, into a historical survey of Western thought, especially Protestantism, rationality, and the flattening of human dignity. The talk closes by asking whether ethics can be understood as something soulful and expansive rather than merely restrictive, and how that changes our sense of blessedness, duty, and what a human life is for.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how ethics, soulmaking, and historical ideas of human nature shape one another.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Rob sets up the question of how Soulmaking Dharma might support ethics and why the talk depends on a sense of what a human being is.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and humanity
chapters.description: He invites listeners to compare ordinary secular self-understanding with moments when the self becomes imaginal and humanity feels deeper and more charged.

chapters.title: Kinds of suffering
chapters.description: Using his chemotherapy experience, he distinguishes bodily pain from soul suffering and argues that ethics must notice different levels of hurt.

chapters.title: Flattening the human
chapters.description: Rob examines utilitarian, voluntarist, and rationalist accounts of ethics, asking how each one shapes or shrinks the human being.

chapters.title: Reformation and rupture
chapters.description: He turns to the Protestant Reformation, the loss of intermediaries, and the cosmic imagery of refraction to show how Western history altered our sense of soul and divinity.

chapters.title: Grace and ethics
chapters.description: Through Luther, Calvin, Melanchthon, and related figures, he traces a historical split between grace and virtue that relegates ethics to civic order.

chapters.title: Telos and soulmaking
chapters.description: He contrasts a grace-only model with Soulmaking Dharma's more balanced view of participation, creation, and a possible human telos toward the divine.

chapters.title: Shame, eros, and dignity
chapters.description: Rob brings in modern pessimism, hiri and ottappa, and the role of shame and guilt, while insisting that ethics also needs love, eros, and soul.

chapters.title: What ethics is for
chapters.description: He ends by asking what kind of image of ethics we live by, and whether Soulmaking Dharma can restore a sense of nobility, blessedness, and a larger purpose for human life.

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea continues the question of how ethics needs some sense of depth or dimensionality in order to feel grounded. He moves through voluntarism, the Protestant Reformation, Augustine, modern utilitarianism, and rationality, asking what each framework takes to be real and how that shapes moral life. The talk gradually turns toward Soulmaking Dharma as a way of re-seeing value, eros, hierarchy, and suffering without collapsing them into a flat, purely instrumental view.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how ethics can be grounded in different kinds of dimensionality, from divine command and cosmic hierarchy to modern rationality and utilitarianism, before turning toward Soulmaking Dharma's participatory alternative.

chapters.title: Ethics and dimensionality
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the problem of grounding ethics in some other dimension and reviews how imaginal figures, angels, and daemons have historically provided that depth.

chapters.title: Voluntarism and scripture
chapters.description: He explains voluntarism as ethics grounded in divine command and scripture, then compares that with Buddhist appeals to scriptural authority.

chapters.title: Reformation and modern flattening
chapters.description: The talk traces how the Protestant Reformation, Ockham, and later secular modernity reshaped ethics around divine sovereignty, ordinary life, and the loss of intermediaries.

chapters.title: Augustine and ordered love
chapters.description: Burbea turns to Augustine's idea that virtue is the order of love and explores how a hierarchical cosmos can support ethical valuation.

chapters.title: Modern cosmos, participatory reality
chapters.description: He contrasts the modern flattened universe with older hierarchies and brings in John Wheeler's participatory universe to suggest a non-naive ontology.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and the world
chapters.description: The talk links sensing soul, eros, and trust in divinity with ethical orientation, then moves through Bacon and Taylor to show how the instrumental stance narrowed modern life.

chapters.title: Rationality and the Logos
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes procedural from substantive rationality, using Plato, Philo, John, Genesis, and Origen to explore reason, Logos, and the soul's longing for truth.

chapters.title: Limits of rational ethics
chapters.description: He questions Enlightenment faith in rationality alone, then tests utilitarian ideas against hierarchy, suffering, and the limits of purely arithmetic moral thinking.

chapters.title: What lies behind reduction
chapters.description: The talk closes by asking whether the wish to reduce suffering may hide a deeper longing for beauty, holiness, and mystery, which Soulmaking Dharma tries to bring into view.

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a wide-ranging inquiry into how ethics can be grounded without collapsing into rules, rights, or mechanical psychology. He argues for expanding ethics toward questions of value, aspiration, and what makes a life worthwhile, then turns to shame, guilt, and the Buddhist teachings of hiri and ottappa as potentially wholesome moral capacities. In the second half, he develops the idea of dimensionality: how imaginal practice, soulmaking, and older religious or philosophical frameworks can restore depth, mediation, and orientation to ethical life.
short_summary: A careful exploration of ethics as value, depth, and soulmaking rather than rule-following alone.

chapters.title: Recap and ethical expansion
chapters.description: He recaps the earlier talk and restates the central move from 'what ought I do?' toward 'what is of value?' and 'what should I love?'.

chapters.title: Worthwhile life questions
chapters.description: Using Hartman and Charles Taylor, he argues that ethics becomes more fertile when it includes questions of the good life, the good to be, and what is worth aspiring to.

chapters.title: Freedom without grounding
chapters.description: He describes the modern freedom from imposed moral ends, but also the loss of a shared basis for deciding what is worthwhile and why.

chapters.title: Against mechanical ethics
chapters.description: He critiques a reduced, Abhidhamma-style picture of ethics as maximizing positive mind moments and minimizing negative ones, which can feel precise but hollow.

chapters.title: Needs of an ethics
chapters.description: He lists several needs for ethics: more than law, room for aspiration, emotion, eros, dimensionality, and a deeper ontology than empty abstraction.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and imaginal practice
chapters.description: He turns to the soulmaking Dharma approach and sketches two ethical applications: working with imaginal figures and working with values or virtues as imaginal ideas.

chapters.title: Shame, guilt, and conscience
chapters.description: He distinguishes shallow shame from soul-level shame, then brings in the Buddha's hiri and ottappa and Buddhaghosa's iron-rod analogy as ethical guardians.

chapters.title: Why ethics needs depth
chapters.description: He argues that ethics needs a higher or deeper dimension to avoid flattening into legalism, reductionism, or purely utilitarian calculation.

chapters.title: Historical sources of dimensionality
chapters.description: He traces ways ethics has been given depth historically, including the Protestant Reformation, angels and intermediaries, rationality, cosmic order, voluntarism, and suffering-based ethics.

title: The Image of Ethics (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens a long argument for rethinking ethics beyond law, rights, and rule-following, using Soulmaking Dharma as a broader basis for moral life. He asks what makes life beautiful, worthwhile, and sacred, and why ethics needs emotion, eros, dimensionality, and a lived sense of value rather than only instructions about what to do. The talk moves through examples from Extinction Rebellion, Nazi Germany, the five precepts, and contemporary moral discourse to show where current frameworks feel thin or distorted.
short_summary: A wide-ranging case for ethics as a lived, soulful orientation toward beauty, value, and responsibility rather than law alone.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Burbea situates the talk as an extension of Sīla and Soul and says he is assuming that background throughout.

chapters.title: Why ethics matters now
chapters.description: He argues that ethics needs reformulation because the current cultural conversation has become too narrow and urgent problems are being missed.

chapters.title: Limits of law and rights
chapters.description: He critiques ethical systems that reduce morality to legality, rights, and competing claims, and he notes their historical and social limits.

chapters.title: What ethics needs
chapters.description: He lays out a wish list for ethics: not just guidance, but beauty, sacredness, a compass-like orientation, and a way of navigating life.

chapters.title: Beyond duty alone
chapters.description: He expands the list to include omission, emotion, love, eros, and a hierarchy of values, while insisting ethics must have a deeper structure.

chapters.title: XR and public rhetoric
chapters.description: Using Extinction Rebellion and airport responses, he shows how law-and-order language can hide the ethical stakes of climate action.

chapters.title: Omission and aspiration
chapters.description: He uses Nazi Germany, the five precepts, and the idea of sins of omission to argue that ethics must include what we fail to do and what we can aspire to.

chapters.title: Virtue, image, and ritual
chapters.description: He revisits earlier Soulmaking material, linking virtue to imaginal form, and reflects on how emotion and ritual can support or obscure ethical depth.

chapters.title: Eros as ethical guidance
chapters.description: He ends by exploring how sensing the soul, discipline, and eros might offer a new epistemology for ethics, one that is neither flatly objective nor merely subjective.

title: Pain, as Void and as Sacrament
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a practical and increasingly imaginal set of ways of working with physical pain in meditation. He begins with simple mindfulness and the easing of reactivity, then moves through insight practices, emptiness-based ways of looking, jhana-based re-perception, and finally soulmaking and ritual. The talk gradually widens from reducing suffering to asking what is most sacred, and how pain can sometimes be met as gift, offering, or divine appearance rather than only as a problem to solve.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on meeting pain through mindfulness, insight, emptiness, jhana, and soulmaking, culminating in a sacramental view of suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the terrain
chapters.description: He sets out the scope of the talk: practical ways of working with physical pain, with some applications to mental dukkha as well.

chapters.title: A progressive list
chapters.description: He explains that he will move from easier, familiar methods toward more difficult but more powerful ones, and notes the relevance of earlier writing in Seeing the Freeze.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and bare attention
chapters.description: He begins with relaxing the body, dropping the story, reducing temporal expansion, and learning to rest with direct sensation.

chapters.title: How pain is fabricated
chapters.description: He deepens the first approach by showing how clinging, labels, and reactivity help fabricate both suffering and, in a stronger sense, the felt intensity of pain itself.

chapters.title: Insight ways of looking
chapters.description: He surveys three characteristics, emptiness, welcoming, metta to phenomena, and related practices that include aversion, attention, and the awareness of awareness itself.

chapters.title: Jhana and malleability
chapters.description: He describes re-seeing pain through jhanic qualities such as piti, happiness, stillness, consciousness, or nothingness, using this to argue for the malleability of vedana.

chapters.title: Why not fully fade?
chapters.description: He asks why one would only partially fade pain, and answers in terms of sacredness, hierarchy, and the relationship between the fabricated and the unfabricated.

chapters.title: Mahayana and Vajrayana frames
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhology, the bodhisattva ideal, and Vajrayana-style appearance as divinity, then links these to the practice of lightly holding emptiness without letting appearances disappear.

chapters.title: Sacrifice and soulmaking
chapters.description: He develops a soulmaking response to pain in which suffering, self, and image are woven into sacrifice, Christ imagery, and a wider imagination of sacredness.

title: The Ontology of the Soul, and the Soul of Ontology ( ... inevitably)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea distinguishes the imaginal middle way from the emptiness middle way, arguing that the first is a broad, practice-based openness to images while the second is the result of a precise ontological inquiry. He then widens the frame into questions of conventional truth, erosion of reductionist psychology, eros, discipline, and the ways our assumptions shape what can be known, valued, and sustained. The talk closes by turning to time itself, suggesting that time may be dependent on the way of looking and asking what that implies for healing, liberation, and death.
short_summary: A careful comparison of imaginal and emptiness-based ontology that expands into eros, conventional truth, and the possibility that time itself is dependently originated.

chapters.title: Two Middle Ways
chapters.description: He opens by distinguishing the ordinary middle way from the ontological middle way in the Kaccayana Sutta.

chapters.title: Imaginal and Emptiness
chapters.description: He contrasts the imaginal middle way with the middle way of emptiness and clarifies why the two are not the same.

chapters.title: Practice and Ontology
chapters.description: He explains why emptiness arises from careful inquiry, while imaginal practice leaves ontology more open and flexible.

chapters.title: Conventional Reality
chapters.description: He turns to conventional truths, ethics, karma, and modern physics to show why emptiness does not settle every ontological question.

chapters.title: Soul and Reductionism
chapters.description: He defines soul as a way of looking, then critiques reductionist materialism and narrow phenomenology as inadequate accounts of human life.

chapters.title: Eros and Disclosure
chapters.description: He develops the idea that ontology can be soulmaking, drawing on John Milbank, Catherine Pickstock, and Descartes to argue that eros can disclose reality.

chapters.title: Conventional Truths at Work
chapters.description: He treats conventional truths as provisional, using examples from science, his own illness, and the language of duty, discipline, and sacrifice.

chapters.title: What Models Open
chapters.description: He tests psychological models by asking what they open or close, and presents the soulmaking dynamic as a useful predictive framework.

chapters.title: Time and Liberation
chapters.description: He closes with caution about shared images, then moves into Plotinus, Alexander Aphrodisias, and the possibility that time is dependently originated.

title: Emptiness and Ways of Looking
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores emptiness through his “ways of looking” approach, arguing that perception, self, time, and suffering are all shaped by how the mind relates to experience. He pauses repeatedly to correct common misunderstandings: that emptiness is a final belief, a bare neutral state, or a license for nihilism. The talk moves from basic definitions to ethics, trauma, imaginal practice, and a careful comparison with quantum physics, while insisting that all of this must remain an experiential inquiry rather than an abstraction.
short_summary: A detailed explanation of emptiness as a practice of changing how perception is shaped, with warnings against reifying emptiness, and reflections on ethics, trauma, and love.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Rob introduces the series, situates it in relation to earlier teachings, and names ontology, emptiness, and ways of looking as the main threads.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He defines the ways-of-looking approach and explains how clinging and fabrication shape what is perceived, including self, world, and time.

chapters.title: No final resting place
chapters.description: He clarifies that there is no moment of consciousness without some way of looking, and warns against imagining a privileged or final lens of truth.

chapters.title: Seeing as, not through
chapters.description: He distinguishes his language of seeing as from other idioms of seeing through, and broadens fabrication from gross papanca to a wide spectrum of non-fabricating.

chapters.title: Subtle distortions
chapters.description: He takes up common misunderstandings about anatta practice, the self, and the unfabricated, especially where aversion or fixed beliefs distort the inquiry.

chapters.title: Practice and flexibility
chapters.description: He presents ways of looking as an art that requires experience and confidence, and shows how emptiness, liberation, love, and soulmaking all depend on it.

chapters.title: Ethics and imagination
chapters.description: He argues that dependent arising and ethics are built into this approach from the beginning, and then turns to imaginal practice and different ontological statuses.

chapters.title: Eros in practice
chapters.description: He says emptiness practice needs eros, curiosity, and real interest, then applies the same open-ended method to trauma rather than choosing between extreme views.

chapters.title: Quantum parallels and awakening
chapters.description: He briefly compares the teaching with quantum physics, returns to the Buddha’s middle way and dependent arising, and ends by asking whether awakening today must include more than emptiness insight alone.

title: PS - Playing in the In-Betweens
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a practical postscript on working skillfully with jhana when experience is partial, changing, or only partly stabilized. He emphasizes a responsive rhythm of practice, the importance of posture, and ways of using imagination to meet pain, dullness, and incomplete absorption without forcing. The talk ends by widening the frame: jhana as a replenishing resource in daily life and as something that can deepen our sense of human and cosmic dimensionality.
short_summary: A practical postscript on responsive jhana practice, subtle posture, and using jhana as a long-term resource.

chapters.title: Opening the Postscript
chapters.description: He explains that this talk is a follow-up to questions that arose during the retreat and introduces the theme of working in grey areas of jhana.

chapters.title: Responsive Practice Rhythm
chapters.description: He argues against rigid sit-walk schedules in favor of attuning practice length and rhythm to what is actually fertile in the moment, including for insight practice.

chapters.title: Posture and Embodiment
chapters.description: He turns to posture, describing it as a subtle support for jhana and sharing stories that show how bodily balance and citta shape each other.

chapters.title: Working with Difficulty
chapters.description: He offers ways to meet pain, fogginess, and other blocks by placing metta, breath, or light at the difficult spot rather than away from it.

chapters.title: Partial Jhana Skills
chapters.description: He discusses what to do when a jhana is present but not fully spread, including pausing, staying with what is already okay, and changing perception.

chapters.title: Moving Between Jhanas
chapters.description: He gives practical guidance on stepping back or forward between jhanas, bringing the breath back in third jhana, and using secondary nimittas to help a state cohere.

chapters.title: Fourth Jhana and Breath
chapters.description: He works with fourth jhana as pure bright awareness, then answers a question about nose and mouth breathing by linking breath subtlety to the subtlety of citta.

chapters.title: Closing Reflections
chapters.description: After a long stretch of garbled closing-list material, he returns to the main thread and explains jhana as a replenishing inner resource for work, service, and steadying life.

chapters.title: Dimensionality of Being
chapters.description: He concludes by saying repeated immersion in jhana changes how one senses human beings and the cosmos, leaving a durable imprint even when the states are no longer accessible.

title: Going Forwards
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a grounded closing talk on how to leave retreat well: appreciating effort without measuring success, caring for the sensitive heart, and carrying practice into ordinary life. He frames practice as a set of streams and playgrounds—jhana, insight, Brahmavihara, emptiness, soulmaking, generosity, right speech—best approached with steady intention, playfulness, and clear curiosity about what one actually desires. The talk ends with a long dedication to the welfare of others and a reminder that practice ripples outward in ways we cannot fully know.
short_summary: A closing retreat talk on carrying practice forward through clear intention, play, and care for the heart.

chapters.title: Appreciation for effort
chapters.description: The talk begins by inviting appreciation for what people have invested in practice, apart from any judgment about results.

chapters.title: Caring for the heart
chapters.description: He turns to the transition off retreat, especially the need to care for the heart, relationships, and the way one shares practice with others.

chapters.title: Steady intention, playful practice
chapters.description: Rob suggests working in a delimited period with one practice, keeping intention steady while allowing experimentation and small creative variations.

chapters.title: Practice streams and mandala
chapters.description: He broadens the picture to different streams of Dharma—insight, samadhi, Brahmavihara, psychology, ethics, activism—and describes them as a coherent mandala.

chapters.title: Balancing the streams
chapters.description: The talk explores how one practice can support another, including balancing insight with samadhi or Brahmavihara and using groups or themed practice periods.

chapters.title: Withdrawal from entanglement
chapters.description: Using the Buddha’s language, he clarifies that jhana depends less on external rules than on not being entangled in hindrances, and he extends this to sex, orgasm, and diet.

chapters.title: Tools and the bookmark
chapters.description: He reminds listeners to keep experimenting with available tools, then uses the image of a bookmark in a book to show how a practiced capacity can remain available over time.

chapters.title: Desire, frameworks, and groups
chapters.description: Rob emphasizes that conceptual frameworks matter, then connects that point to desire, intention, and the different ways practices and groups can succeed or stall.

chapters.title: Group intentions clarified
chapters.description: He contrasts the needs of soulmaking, sangha, and other group forms, showing how unclear desire can distort a group’s purpose and outcome.

title: The End of Time (The Cessation of Perception and Feeling)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the unfabricated as something beyond ordinary perception, time, and the familiar sense-world, then turns to how it can be approached through jhana and insight ways of looking. He distinguishes this from simply being calm, bare, or unthinking, and spends much of the talk clarifying how fabrication, clinging, emptiness, and dependent origination fit together. The talk also pauses for philosophical questions about what can be trusted as knowledge, and for a few Pali Canon passages that frame the practice in unusually detailed terms.
short_summary: A careful exploration of the unfabricated, showing how deep jhana and insight can reveal fabrication, emptiness, and the subtle co-arising of subject, object, and time.

chapters.title: Opening context
chapters.description: Rob sets the frame for a late-retreat talk and says he wants to focus on the larger conceptual picture rather than technical details alone.

chapters.title: Unfabricated and emptiness
chapters.description: He introduces the unfabricated and explains why it matters alongside emptiness, then gives a brief distinction between dependent origination, emptiness, and lack of inherent existence.

chapters.title: Dependent origination
chapters.description: Using a Pali Canon story of the Buddha and Ananda, he argues that dependent origination is profound, not obvious, and that the mind’s way of looking is central to liberation.

chapters.title: What the unfabricated means
chapters.description: He clarifies that the unfabricated is not merely calm attention or nonthinking, but something much more radical, and notes how rare this framing is in Dharma teaching.

chapters.title: Beyond perception
chapters.description: Several sutta passages are read to show a state where all perception, sense contact, and ordinary time-space have ceased, including the language of consciousness without attribute.

chapters.title: Experience and knowledge
chapters.description: An aside on epistemology and ontology opens into the question of what meditation can legitimately show us about reality, and how philosophy and practice meet.

chapters.title: Jhana as insight
chapters.description: Rob reads passages where jhana is seen as fabricated, impermanent, and empty, then explains why stable absorption can become a strong basis for insight.

chapters.title: Sariputta’s discernment
chapters.description: A passage on Sāriputta shows jhana used with great skill in insight ways of looking, discerning the arising and fading of qualities without attraction or repulsion.

chapters.title: Subject, object, time
chapters.description: He develops a subtler account of fabrication in which subject, object, and time arise together, carrying intention, craving, and the basic momentum of samsara.

title: The Eighth Jhana (The Realm of Neither Perception Nor Non-Perception)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the eighth jhana as a state where consciousness no longer lands on any object, not even nothingness. He spends much of the talk clarifying what perception means, arguing that it is not verbal labeling but the fabricating of an object for consciousness, and he uses that definition to place the realm of neither perception nor non-perception within the wider arc of meditative refinement. The talk moves from jhana theory into practice instructions, then widens into a careful warning about aversion, dependent arising, emptiness, and why the conceptual framework around insight matters as much as the experience itself.
short_summary: A careful explanation of the eighth jhana as non-landing consciousness, framed through perception, fabrication, and dependent arising.

chapters.title: Entering the eighth jhana
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the move beyond the realm of nothingness into neither perception nor non-perception and reads the canonical framing of the state.

chapters.title: What perception means
chapters.description: He begins redefining perception, arguing that the key issue is not labeling but how an object is constituted for consciousness.

chapters.title: Perception as fabrication
chapters.description: The talk develops the central interpretive move: perception, experience, appearance, and object are treated as closely related terms for fabrication.

chapters.title: Nothingness as last object
chapters.description: He contrasts the realm of nothingness with the eighth jhana, where even the subtle object of nothingness is no longer being landed on.

chapters.title: Non-landing and release
chapters.description: Using Hitchhiker's Guide and a sutta analogy about sunlight, he describes the felt sense of not landing, being unhooked, and the liberation that comes with it.

chapters.title: Refinement through the jhanas
chapters.description: He situates the eighth jhana within a spectrum of increasing refinement, then offers practice suggestions for moving into it through subtle attention and maturation.

chapters.title: Two paths into the state
chapters.description: He turns to the Anupada Sutta and outlines several ways the state may open, including insight reflections, a subtle question, or tuning to a hidden level of mind.

chapters.title: Insight without aversion
chapters.description: Burbea warns that genuine disenchantment differs from repulsion, and he contrasts liberating insight with practice that has become agitated or aversive.

chapters.title: Emptiness and intimacy
chapters.description: He closes by showing how fabrication, dependent arising, and emptiness can lead not to dualism but to a more intimate, tender, and wonder-filled relation to experience.

title: The Seventh Jhana (The Realm of Nothingness)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the seventh jhana as the dimension of nothingness, starting with the Buddha’s description and then moving into the practical problems of how it is recognized, entered, and trusted. He emphasizes that this territory is often frightening unless it grows out of earlier steadier absorptions, and he keeps returning to the difference between mere absorption and liberating insight. The talk then widens into emptiness, perception, desire, heart-opening, and finally the imaginal possibilities that can open when perception becomes more malleable.
short_summary: A close, practical reading of the realm of nothingness, linking jhana, emptiness, perception, and the shaping power of desire.

chapters.title: Introducing Nothingness
chapters.description: Burbea names the seventh jhana and frames it through the Buddha’s description of transcending infinite consciousness into nothingness.

chapters.title: Unexpected Openings
chapters.description: He describes how people may stumble into a void-like experience without a map, and why that can be confusing or frightening.

chapters.title: Stepping Stones
chapters.description: The talk turns to the earlier jhanas as stable bases that make the movement into nothingness less abrupt and less alarming.

chapters.title: Insight Ways of Looking
chapters.description: Burbea uses the Ananja Sappaya Sutta to show how seeing perceptions as just perceptions and as not peaceful can open the dimension of nothingness.

chapters.title: Empty of Self
chapters.description: He develops the sutta’s third contemplation, explaining how this is not just about personal self but about phenomenal self and fabricated things.

chapters.title: Confidence in the Dimension
chapters.description: The Buddha’s formula about practicing and frequently abiding becomes a way of explaining how confidence in nothingness is gradually established.

chapters.title: No Thingness and Fabrication
chapters.description: Burbea works through different subtle ways of looking at experience, insisting that the key payoff is insight into no-things rather than simple impermanence.

chapters.title: From Samadhi to Insight
chapters.description: He argues that insight is more important than the sheer degree of absorption and shows how this understanding changes the way one moves in the world.

chapters.title: Heart, Desire, and Limits
chapters.description: The talk broadens to metta, desire, and the power of concepts to either open or constrain what the heart can do.

title: The Sixth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Consciousness)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the sixth jhana as the realm of infinite consciousness, reading the Buddha’s brief description and unpacking it as a trainable shift into awareness knowing itself. He distinguishes this from the vastness of awareness, then widens the talk into questions of provisional truth, dependent origination, and how meditation changes perception after the formal sit. The talk closes with practical notes on access, the role of desire, and a brief aside on how imaginal practice can be used tactically when a retreat calls for it.
short_summary: A careful explanation of the sixth jhana as infinite consciousness, with practice instructions and a comparison to the vastness of awareness.

chapters.title: Opening the Topic
chapters.description: The talk is introduced as a look at the sixth jhana, named here in the Buddha’s language as the realm of infinite consciousness.

chapters.title: Canonical Description
chapters.description: Rob reads the short canonical passage and treats it as an instruction to enter, stay with, and establish consciousness itself.

chapters.title: Awareness Knows Itself
chapters.description: He develops the core claim that awareness and consciousness are the same here, and that the key feature is awareness knowing awareness.

chapters.title: How Attention Settles
chapters.description: Subtle internal cues, repeated noticing, and tuning the mind toward the sense of knowing are presented as the way the state is stabilized and amplified.

chapters.title: Intensity and Release
chapters.description: He describes the realm as electric, self-sufficient, and transcendent, emphasizing its sense of escape from the phenomenal world.

chapters.title: Vastness Compared
chapters.description: Rob compares the sixth jhana with the vastness of awareness, distinguishing a state that excludes phenomena from one that includes them more fully.

chapters.title: After-Effects and Truth
chapters.description: The discussion turns to the after-effects on perception, the rarity of the states, and the caution that these are deep but still provisional truths.

chapters.title: Paths In and View
chapters.description: He outlines several ways into the realm, situates the formless attainments in the wider Buddhist map, and links them to release and dependent origination.

chapters.title: Training, Desire, and Use
chapters.description: The final stretch emphasizes practice as training, the importance of desire and coherent intention, and a brief tactical note on using imaginal or insight approaches when needed.

title: The Fifth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Space)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the fifth jhana as the realm of infinite space, moving from the Buddha’s brief sutta description into a careful account of how perception shifts when solidity, resistance, and attention to diversity fall away. He then widens the talk into related themes: the role of metta and compassion in the higher jhanas, the practical use of insight ways of looking, and the way boundless space can change how body, world, and self are perceived. In the latter part of the talk he turns to desire, asking what a person truly wants, how that shapes practice, and how Soulmaking ideas can help sustain an authentic path.
short_summary: A detailed teaching on infinite space, with practical meditation advice, related sutta references, and a long turn toward desire, authenticity, and Soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the fifth jhana
chapters.description: Rob introduces the realm of infinite space and frames it as a topic that may feel remote or immediate depending on the listener.

chapters.title: The Buddha's description
chapters.description: He reads the sutta passage closely and explains the sequence of transcending form, resistance, and diversity before entering infinite space.

chapters.title: What space means
chapters.description: Rob unpacks the language of formlessness, subtle materiality, and the cosmological map that links jhana states with realms of experience.

chapters.title: How it opens
chapters.description: He compares the Visuddhimagga kasina method with his own approach, emphasizing that space opens by noticing non-solidity rather than by forcing expansion.

chapters.title: Fourth to fifth jhana
chapters.description: Rob describes the relationship between the fourth jhana and the formless realms, including the role of stillness, release, and secondary signs such as delight and oneness.

chapters.title: Metta and higher realms
chapters.description: He argues that metta and compassion can extend beyond the lower jhanas and can be mixed with infinite space and related states.

chapters.title: Insight as a way of looking
chapters.description: Using a Majjhima Nikaya sutta, he shows how impermanence, dukkha, and elemental perception can function as lived ways of looking that open into non-solidity.

chapters.title: Presence and worldviews
chapters.description: Rob questions the modern ideal of simply being present, showing how it rests on hidden assumptions about practice, value, and the world.

chapters.title: Desire, authenticity, and practice
chapters.description: The talk expands into a long reflection on authentic desire, cultural conditioning, Soulmaking, and the self on the path, before returning briefly to infinite space and closing.

title: Jhanas and Insight
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the relationship between jhana and insight as a single continuum rather than a simple two-step path. He questions common models of concentration and awakening, then develops a view in which perception, selfing, clinging, and dukkha all vary together across a spectrum of fabrication. The talk moves from familiar Buddhist formulas to emptiness, dependent origination, and the practical after-effects of meditative states on ordinary perception.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how jhana and insight can support one another through the malleability of perception, self, and fabrication.

chapters.title: Framing the Question
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as an inquiry into how jhanas and insight relate, and explains why the question takes repeated reflection to understand.

chapters.title: Questioning the Usual Formula
chapters.description: He challenges the common idea that jhana or concentration must come first and insight second, reading the usual sequence as a contextual teaching device rather than a literal recipe.

chapters.title: Competing Models of Awakening
chapters.description: Rob lays out several traditional ways of understanding awakening, including reducing papanca, greed, aversion, and delusion, and asks where jhana fits in each model.

chapters.title: Emptiness and Ways of Looking
chapters.description: He shifts to emptiness as a deep embodied knowing, and explains insight practice as experimenting with ways of looking that change how phenomena, self, and time are experienced.

chapters.title: One Spectrum of Fabrication
chapters.description: Rob argues that jhana, dukkha, self-sense, and object-sense all lie on one continuum, so that less fabrication in one dimension tends to affect the others.

chapters.title: Practice, Agency, and Caution
chapters.description: A retreat note complicates the picture by showing that some forms of practice can restore agency and psychological balance, reminding listeners that not every healing comes from jhana alone.

chapters.title: After-Effects on Perception
chapters.description: He introduces the idea that meditative states leave lingering effects on perception, illustrated with a participant’s account of seeing the world as radiant and joyful.

chapters.title: Dependent Origination in Practice
chapters.description: Rob uses metta, equanimity, and the language of lawfulness to show how changing the way attention meets experience alters what is fabricated and what fades.

chapters.title: Jhana and Insight Both Ways
chapters.description: He maps the twelve links of dependent origination onto his spectrum of fabrication, then shows how specific jhanas and specific insight views can lead into one another, ending with a warning about getting fixed in any view as ultimate.

title: Q & A
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea moves from practical distinctions in jhana practice to a wider discussion of how attention shapes experience. He answers questions about pīti and sukha, how to work with primary nimitta versus side qualities, and how to tell when to stay with jhana and when a different practice is more appropriate. In the later part of the talk, he turns toward insight practice, dependent arising, and a reading of the Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna teachings that emphasizes how perception is fabricated rather than merely observed.
short_summary: A Q&A on jhana refinement, the balance of opening and probing, and a deeper reading of mindfulness as dependent arising.

chapters.title: Opening questions
chapters.description: Rob opens the Q&A and frames the first question about jhana terminology and practice experience.

chapters.title: Primary nimitta and side qualities
chapters.description: He distinguishes the main jhana sign from nearby heart qualities like love, devotion, and nobility, and explains why the retreat stays on the jhana path.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and practice scope
chapters.description: Rob contrasts bounded dharma training with the open-ended field of soulmaking, then returns to questions about pīti, sukha, and refinement within jhana.

chapters.title: Pīti, sukha, and self
chapters.description: He clarifies bodily pervasion, the range within each jhana factor, and how less self-identification can naturally appear in second jhana.

chapters.title: Insight and concentration
chapters.description: Rob cautions against applying insight too early to jhana and describes how a stable concentration can later become an object for insight ways of looking.

chapters.title: Opening, probing, and steadiness
chapters.description: He works through how receptive openness and directed probing interact, using lake-diving and other images to show why the balance is fluid rather than formulaic.

chapters.title: Sustaining intensity
chapters.description: Rob addresses the tendency to probe too hard or to seek pleasure too passively, and suggests cultivating intensity without grasping while noticing how attention affects pain.

chapters.title: Ways of looking at pain
chapters.description: He develops the point that mindfulness is never bare, showing how different factors in attention can fabricate or unfabricate pain and how this opens into insight practice.

chapters.title: Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna
chapters.description: Rob rereads the Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna teachings as instructions about the arising and dissolution of experience, ending with a quiet closing and tea.

title: The Fourth Jhana
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the fourth jhana as a state of refined stillness, equanimity, and bright whole-body awareness, moving slowly through the Buddha’s canonical description and then into lived experience. He spends time on what makes this state distinct from the third jhana, how it can feel luminous or even like another world, and why the language of purity, brightness, and imperturbability matters. Later he widens the talk toward perception, dependent origination, emotional life, and how jhana practice can support insight without being reduced to a rigid formula.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of the fourth jhana as luminous stillness, deep equanimity, and a basis for insight practice.

chapters.title: From Third to Fourth
chapters.description: He begins by distinguishing the third jhana’s tender peacefulness from the deeper refinement of the fourth.

chapters.title: Canonical Description
chapters.description: He reads the Buddha’s description of the fourth jhana and translates it into experiential language of pure, bright awareness.

chapters.title: Stillness and Brightness
chapters.description: He unpacks stillness, white light, and the sense that this state can feel like another realm or be called 'the beautiful.'

chapters.title: Perception and Emptiness
chapters.description: He broadens the teaching into how jhanas reveal the malleability of perception and point toward dependent origination and emptiness.

chapters.title: Equanimity Deepens
chapters.description: He contrasts equanimity with multiplicity against the deeper singleness of the fourth jhana, where almost nothing remains to react to.

chapters.title: Emotion and Intention
chapters.description: He discusses subtle metta and compassion within the fourth jhana and connects it to right resolve on the path.

chapters.title: Wrapped in Stillness
chapters.description: He develops the image of being wrapped in a white cloth and then in 360-degree stillness, including the common experience of leaning forward.

chapters.title: Purity, Malleability, Insight
chapters.description: He unpacks the post-jhana list as qualities of mind—pure, bright, workable, steady, imperturbable—and treats it as preparation for vipassana rather than a rigid sequence.

chapters.title: Base Practice and Maturing
chapters.description: He closes by discussing Ajahn Lee’s advice about using the fourth jhana as a base, his own preference for including the third, and the slow maturing of mastery.

title: Q & A
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: This Q&A moves from a practical request to keep silence on retreat into a long, detailed discussion of desire, intention, equanimity, jhāna, and the imaginal life. Rob Burbea repeatedly returns to the idea that Dharma terms work best as spectra rather than fixed categories, and he uses that lens to talk about clinging, self, emotion, and the energy body. The talk also distinguishes jhāna practice from Soulmaking Dharma, especially around eros, two-ness, and dissolution, while warning against spiritual bypassing and vague uses of equanimity.
short_summary: A wide-ranging retreat Q&A on silence, desire, equanimity, jhāna, the energy body, and the difference between imaginal practice and absorption.

chapters.title: Silence on retreat
chapters.description: Rob begins by asking the group to keep silence outside the formal Q&A and explains silence as a way to let sensitivity and connection deepen.

chapters.title: Goal and intention
chapters.description: He answers a question about jhāna and shows how intention involves letting go of clinging, aversion, and the usual ways we chase goals.

chapters.title: Desire and devotion
chapters.description: The talk turns to the gravity of desire, self-doubt, and the difference between shallow wishes and deeper currents of devotion and Eros.

chapters.title: What equanimity means
chapters.description: Rob unpacks equanimity as equipoise, equal seeing, and non-grasping, then expands the teaching from worldly balance into deeper meditative refinement.

chapters.title: Clinging as a spectrum
chapters.description: He reframes push-pull and clinging as continuous and subtle, showing how deeper jhāna and insight reveal finer layers rather than a simple on-off switch.

chapters.title: Desire, fabrication, and practice
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes between useful and unhelpful ways of seeing desire as fabricated, and encourages learning from what moves, weakens, or sustains desire over time.

chapters.title: Pīti, sukha, and the body
chapters.description: He explains jhānic pleasure as whole-body, energetic experience and answers questions about emotion, bodily feeling, and the practical use of the energy body.

chapters.title: Jhāna and soulmaking
chapters.description: The later discussion contrasts jhāna with Soulmaking Dharma: imagery and eros keep polarity, while jhāna tends toward dissolution and less duality.

chapters.title: Bypass and closing questions
chapters.description: Rob closes by warning that no single practice is complete, then returns to how jhāna can reveal emotional patterns without simply suppressing them.

title: Q & A, and Short Talk
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: This talk moves from a practical question about working with emotions in jhana practice into a wide-ranging set of clarifications about intention, perception, and emptiness. Rob Burbea keeps returning to the difference between what is skillful in a retreat setting and what belongs in the wider arc of practice and life, with repeated emphasis on context, lightness, and not getting locked into a narrow goal. Later he expands into jhana mastery, bodily and emotional “locks,” and the deeper point that perception itself is dependently arisen and empty. The talk ends with a quieter note on how jhana factors can spill into ordinary life, without making happiness or release into a small trick of comfort management.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how emotions, jhana, perception, and emptiness fit together, while warning against turning practice into a narrow project of control or pain management.

chapters.title: Context and intention
chapters.description: Rob begins by framing emotional work inside the larger context of retreat, personal practice, and ordinary life, and says the retreat’s central intention must stay steady.

chapters.title: Tears and openness
chapters.description: He distinguishes sadness from being touched, moved, or open-hearted, and suggests that tears and sensitivity can be part of the path rather than signs of collapse.
