chapters.description: Burbea argues that the real point is not fascinating imagery, but the opening, healing, and re-enchantment of existence itself.

chapters.title: Philosophy and Examples
chapters.description: He quotes J. N. Findlay on mysticism and beauty, then uses examples from music and lovers to show that sensing with soul can be embodied and ordinary.

chapters.title: Soul in All Senses
chapters.description: Using B.B. King, touch, thinking, and the six or seven senses, he shows how the whole field of experience can be sensed imaginally.

chapters.title: Four Modes of Contact
chapters.description: He outlines everyday, dharma-lensed, oneness-based, and imaginal modes of sense contact, clarifying what changes in each one.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Dependence
chapters.description: Burbea links sensing with soul to mindfulness, appropriate attention, and dependent origination, emphasizing responsiveness rather than passivity.

chapters.title: Reverence and Play
chapters.description: He closes by showing that reverence, discernment, and play are part of sensing with soul and of practice itself.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 6)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: In this part of the Imaginal series, Rob Burbea revisits the basic aim of the teaching: to refine how the imaginal is noticed, amplified, and supported in practice. He moves through several qualities that help an image become more fully imaginal, including meaningfulness, timelessness, the imaginal middle way, and participation. The talk stays close to practice, returning again and again to how concepts, perception, and the way of looking shape what is experienced.
short_summary: Rob Burbea outlines several qualities that help imaginal perception become more vivid, nuanced, and workable in practice.

chapters.title: Framing the Teaching
chapters.description: He restates the purpose of the series and the practical reason for naming the qualities of the imaginal.

chapters.title: Amplifying the Image
chapters.description: He explains three ways to work with an imaginal node: notice it, question it, or deliberately turn it on.

chapters.title: Subtlety and Intention
chapters.description: He emphasizes delicacy in practice and gives examples of humility, love, and fullness of intention as catalysts.

chapters.title: Bodies of Light and Jewel
chapters.description: He offers deliberate imaginal images of the body, including body as light, mantra as jewels, and body as earth.

chapters.title: Meaningfulness and Devotion
chapters.description: He describes meaningfulness as larger than any single meaning, with a sense of pregnant significance and orientation.

chapters.title: Timelessness and Sacred Time
chapters.description: He develops the sense of eternality, contrast it with narrative time, and shows how it can open the imaginal world.

chapters.title: Holding Impermanence
chapters.description: He guards against misunderstanding timelessness as denial of impermanence and insists both dimensions can be held together.

chapters.title: Imaginal Middle Way
chapters.description: He distinguishes the imaginal middle way from ordinary realist categories and connects it to the theatre-like quality of imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Way of Looking
chapters.description: He explains that the imaginal depends on the perceiver's way of looking and that this dependence does not make it unreal.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: In this installment, Rob Burbea turns to what he calls fullness of intention, arguing that imaginal practice matures when it moves beyond self-improvement toward soul-making, divinity, and Buddha-nature. He then develops two linked themes: the autonomy of the imaginal other within dependent arising, and the way imaginal perception preserves two-ness rather than collapsing into oneness. In the later part of the talk, he connects indefinite boundaries, infinite echoing, meaning, and values, showing how images become fertile when they are not reduced to a single interpretation.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores fullness of intention, autonomy, and meaning in imaginal practice, moving from soul-making to boundaries, echoes, and values.

chapters.title: Fullness of Intention
chapters.description: Burbea defines the 12th element as an intention that moves beyond self-growth toward soul-making, divinity, and Buddha-nature.

chapters.title: Autonomy and Dependent Arising
chapters.description: He introduces the imaginal other as autonomous while keeping dependent arising as the underlying Buddhist frame.

chapters.title: Dartmoor Tentacles
chapters.description: A walk on Dartmoor becomes an example of an image that is rapturous without meaning union or dissolution.

chapters.title: Two-ness and Free Choice
chapters.description: He extends the discussion to self and other, then brings in freedom, necessity, and the role of intention in soulmaking.

chapters.title: Ethics and Freedom
chapters.description: Viktor Frankl’s testimony is used to show how ethical choice can remain present even under extreme constraint.

chapters.title: Soft Edges of Being
chapters.description: Burbea develops the 14th element: boundaries in imaginal perception are indefinite, soft, and elastic rather than fixed.

chapters.title: Echoing and Resonance
chapters.description: He links soft boundaries to the sense of infinite echoing, then illustrates how an image can mirror a person’s life and practice.

chapters.title: Meaning and Values
chapters.description: The final stretch clarifies that meaningfulness includes, but is not limited to, specific meanings, and it ends by tying imaginal life to values and their open-endedness.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues unfolding imaginal practice by treating it as a constellation of elements rather than a single faculty. He moves through how different nodes can ignite the whole field, including eros, beauty, divinity, grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty, while staying careful not to turn any of these into rigid doctrines. The talk keeps returning to a middle way: not reducing the imaginal to psychology or imagination, and not reifying it into a fixed metaphysics.
short_summary: A careful exploration of the elements that make imaginal perception alive, including grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty.

chapters.title: Imaginal constellation
chapters.description: Burbea frames imaginal perception as a lattice of interrelated elements that can be studied, tuned, and gently stirred.

chapters.title: Ignition and grace
chapters.description: He describes how one node can light up the whole field, and how receptivity matters as much as technique.

chapters.title: Switches and dials
chapters.description: The talk develops the sense that imaginal practice can shift suddenly or gradually, with attention acting like a set of adjustable dials.

chapters.title: Elements of the field
chapters.description: He names several key nodes in the constellation, including energy body, soulfulness, love, eros, and beauty.

chapters.title: Dimensionality and divinity
chapters.description: Burbea explores how imaginal depth shades into divinity, while warning against easy ontological claims and insisting on an epistemological middle way.

chapters.title: Grace, reverence, humility
chapters.description: He describes the sacred as gift, then turns to reverence, bowing, and humility as bodily and ethical orientations rather than self-effacing ideals.

chapters.title: Humility as image
chapters.description: Humility itself becomes an imaginal object that can expand, deepen, and be transformed through soulmaking rather than remaining a fixed virtue.

chapters.title: Trust in the image
chapters.description: A grain of trust can loosen reification, let puzzling images move, and reveal a deeper intelligence at work in the field.

chapters.title: Duty to consolidate
chapters.description: The talk closes with duty as the soul’s felt responsibility to bring new insight, oneness, or practice-state into ordinary life and not let it be forgotten.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea continues refining the idea of the imaginal by stressing that it is relational rather than something any image or object possesses on its own. He develops the image of a lattice or constellation of linked factors—especially energy body awareness, soul-making, and love—showing how attention to one node can awaken the others. The talk stays practical throughout, balancing disciplined attention with a sense that imaginal life also involves grace, newness, and something not fully under technique.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explains the imaginal as a relational lattice of practice, where energy body, soul-making, and love can mutually ignite one another.

chapters.title: Recap of the framework
chapters.description: He reviews the loose, non-exhaustive list of imaginal aspects and emphasizes that the teaching is meant to sharpen discernment without becoming rigid.

chapters.title: Relational, not inherent
chapters.description: Using James Hillman as a comparison, he argues that nothing is inherently imaginal; it becomes so through a particular relationship and what that relationship opens.

chapters.title: The imaginal lattice
chapters.description: He introduces the image of a lattice of nodes, explaining that when enough elements are alive together, the whole perception can become imaginal.

chapters.title: Wiggling a node
chapters.description: He gives practical suggestions for nudging practice along the spectrum, especially by noticing and enlivening the energy body as one node in the constellation.

chapters.title: What energy body means
chapters.description: He defines energy body as a way of looking, contrasts it with materialist conceptions, and shows how body, concept, image, and felt sense overlap.

chapters.title: Soul-making and jhana
chapters.description: He turns to soul-making, saying it involves eros, psyche, and logos, and illustrates it with jhana practice and the deepening sense of connection to the tradition.

chapters.title: New territory and play
chapters.description: He explores how newness, body awareness, heart practice, and playfulness can themselves become soul-making when they open fresh range and meaning.

chapters.title: Love as a key node
chapters.description: He ends by focusing on loving and being loved as a decisive node in the imaginal lattice, including the different flavors love can take and the value of affirming it.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues clarifying what he means by the imaginal: not just inner pictures, but a way of looking that softens fabrication and opens perception to meaning, beauty, and soul resonance. He distinguishes imaginal practice from helpful but non-imaginal uses of imagination, then names duty, trust, and selfless intention as part of the terrain. The talk then turns practical, with many ways into the imaginal, a long personal example, and guidance for noticing when ordinary life is already being seen imaginally.
short_summary: A practical and nuanced talk about how imaginal perception differs from ordinary imagination, and how to approach it without forcing images.

chapters.title: Recapping the terrain
chapters.description: He reviews the spectrum of imagination and re-sets the basic distinction between useful imagination and the imaginal.

chapters.title: Duty and trust
chapters.description: He adds duty, trust, and non-self-centered intention to the constellation of factors that support imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Opening the gates
chapters.description: He briefly points listeners to earlier instructions and then turns to how one might begin working with images in practice.

chapters.title: Ways into image
chapters.description: He offers a wide set of entry points: samadhi, emptiness, dreams, prior figures, deities, people, places, emotion, and the body.

chapters.title: The black coat story
chapters.description: A plain image of suit lapels unfolds into a fuller imaginal sense of a man, his devotion, and the meaning carried by the scene.

chapters.title: Vivid but not imaginal
chapters.description: He contrasts the previous example with a dramatic snake image that lacked trust, love, and dimensionality at first.

chapters.title: Patient transformation
chapters.description: He shows how introducing trust and related qualities can change an image over time, and reminds listeners that imaginal images are not always visual.

chapters.title: Technique and patience
chapters.description: He speaks candidly about not being naturally image-rich, and encourages experimentation, patience, and alternating imaginal work with other practices.

chapters.title: Life already imaginal
chapters.description: He closes by pointing to the imaginal already present in ordinary life, where self, body, conversation, inquiry, and world can all become image through the right mode of being.

title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: 'The Mirrored Gates' is a set of talks (recorded by Rob from his home) attempting to clarify, elaborate on, and open up further the concepts, practices, and possibilities explained in previous talks on imaginal practice. Some working familiarity with those previous teachings will provide a helpful foundation for this new set; but a good understanding of and experiential facility with practices of emptiness, samatha, the emotional/energy body, metta, and mindfulness is necessary and presumed, without which these new teachings may be confusing and difficult to comprehend.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens a new short course on the imaginal by situating it as a clarification of earlier soulmaking teachings rather than a new system. He distinguishes ordinary imagination, papañca, metta imagery, and healing images from what he means by "imaginal," then introduces "sensing with soul" as an alternate way in. The talk ends by outlining a careful spectrum from ordinary imagination toward the fuller imaginal, with attention to the role of love, eros, beauty, autonomy, humility, grace, and soul-making.
short_summary: A careful opening talk that defines the imaginal as sensing with soul, places it on a spectrum from ordinary imagination, and names the qualities that make it deepen practice.

chapters.title: Course framing
chapters.description: Rob frames the series as a supplement to earlier soulmaking talks and emphasizes pacing, digestion, and clarification over adding new material.

chapters.title: What imaginal means
chapters.description: He begins to define the imaginal and introduces "sensing with soul" as nearly interchangeable language that may make the teaching easier to hear.

chapters.title: Different practitioner entry points
chapters.description: Rob sketches three practitioner situations: those who do not get images, those unsure whether their images are imaginal, and those who sense the sacred in the world but not inner images.

chapters.title: Ontology and knowing
chapters.description: He turns briefly to what is real and how we know, arguing that imaginal practice questions ordinary assumptions about ontology and epistemology.

chapters.title: Imagination in practice
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes papañca from mindful, embodied imagination and gives examples from metta and healing imagery, including figures such as Tara, the Buddha, Jesus, or a grandmother.

chapters.title: Naming the tradition
chapters.description: He pauses to say that his use of imaginal is specific, noting overlap and difference with Henri Corbin, James Hillman, Tom Cheetham, Philip Pullman, and Mary Watkins, then begins listing core qualities.

chapters.title: Core qualities of the imaginal
chapters.description: Rob names a set of qualities such as beyondness, autonomy, two-ness, mutual mirroring, meaningfulness, theater, conceptual structure, timelessness, humility, reverence, grace, and soulfulness.

chapters.title: Why lists matter
chapters.description: He explains why the list is useful despite his ambivalence, using the Buddha as a model for enumerated teaching and saying these elements can act as ignition keys for practice.

chapters.title: A dynamic spectrum
chapters.description: The talk closes by stressing that imaginal experience is dynamic rather than fixed, and that the question is how to move toward the fuller, more fertile end of the spectrum.

title: Shaping Vessels
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea closes a retreat by asking what actually supports practice once people leave the retreat container. He reflects on loneliness, vulnerability, and the difficulty of sharing what feels most alive, then explores vessels for practice: silent retreat, study, dyads, imaginal sharing, and careful speech. The talk gradually turns toward how intimacy and mutuality can sanctify experience and help practice keep growing in ordinary life.
short_summary: A closing retreat talk on the vessels that sustain practice off-retreat, from shared silence to imaginal exchange and right speech.

chapters.title: Opening Thanks
chapters.description: He begins by thanking the group for their practice and the quality of their participation in the retreat.

chapters.title: Practice Beyond Retreat
chapters.description: He asks what supports practice, growth, and soulmaking after retreat ends, framing the central question of the talk.

chapters.title: Loneliness In Dharma
chapters.description: He reflects on how dedicated practitioners can still feel isolated, especially when what matters most is hard to share.

chapters.title: Vulnerability And Simplicity
chapters.description: A story about a dog opening her belly becomes a contrast to human guardedness and complexity.

chapters.title: Kinds Of Vessel
chapters.description: He names different containers for practice, including retreat, study, discussion, and the sharing of images.

chapters.title: Temenos For Images
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of temenos, or sacred holding, and shows how structured sharing can deepen imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Right Speech Reconsidered
chapters.description: He turns to right speech as something that shapes consciousness and perception, not just conduct.

chapters.title: Closing Question
chapters.description: He ends by returning to the practical question of what will support the life of practice going forward.

title: Beauty and the Buddha
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores beauty as more than pleasant sense experience, tracing it from the Pali Canon and the fourth jhana into eros, soulmaking, imaginal perception, and divinity. He contrasts that wider field with views that reduce beauty to impermanence, tragedy, or mere craving, and argues that both emptiness practice and imaginal practice can open perception rather than narrow it. The talk moves quietly but insistently toward a nondual, participatory understanding of self, world, and sense experience.
short_summary: A wide-ranging reflection on beauty, eros, emptiness, and imaginal perception as forms of soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the Question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces beauty as the talk’s theme and says the material will unfold nonlinearly, tied to earlier retreat teachings.

chapters.title: Beauty in the Canon
chapters.description: He looks at the Pali Canon’s unusual use of beauty, especially its association with the mind and the fourth jhana rather than ordinary sense objects.

chapters.title: Sense Pleasures and Limits
chapters.description: The talk turns to the canon’s warnings about sense pleasure and the tension between worldly senses and the unfabricated.

chapters.title: Radiance and Vividness
chapters.description: He considers a friend’s claim that beauty arises from mindful, craving-free perception, then reframes radiance as vividness, alertness, and presence.

chapters.title: Tragedy or More
chapters.description: Keats’s negative capability and a tragic reading of art are used as a contrast, setting up Burbea’s critique of a singular, impermanence-centered aesthetic.

chapters.title: Eros and Resonance
chapters.description: Burbea develops beauty as eros, resonance, and participation between subject and object, linking it to imaginal experience and the widening of perception.

chapters.title: Emptiness and Dimensions
chapters.description: He argues that beauty is relational and empty of fixed essence, then critiques practices or teachings that flatten experience into one dimension or one taste.

chapters.title: Divinity and Buddha Nature
chapters.description: Beauty becomes an analogy for divinity and buddhanature, presented as open-ended, mysterious, and available through shifts in ways of looking.

chapters.title: Soulmaking Practice
chapters.description: He closes by describing how soulmaking, emptiness, and an expanded sense of the senses can dissolve dualism and orient practice toward limitless creation and discovery.

title: Modes of Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: This Q&A explores how emptiness, samadhi, and imaginal practice can converge without collapsing into a reductive view. Rob Burbea argues that emptiness need not be deflationary; it can open into mystery, divinity, and a wider field of mystical perception. The conversation then moves through cosmopoiesis, the spread of imaginal qualities, the role of eros, and how this approach differs from more prescribed forms of Tantra.
short_summary: Rob Burbea discusses emptiness, samadhi, eros, and imaginal practice as converging paths that can open perception rather than reduce it.

chapters.title: Emptiness and divinity
chapters.description: He contrasts a deflationary reading of emptiness with an understanding of emptiness as a doorway into mystery, divinity, and multiple kinds of mystical experience.

chapters.title: Samadhi and less fabrication
chapters.description: Rob explains that samadhi and emptiness are related by progressively less fabrication of self, other, and world, and places the classical lists of practice inside that movement.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis explained
chapters.description: He defines cosmopoiesis as the way perception helps create the cosmos in a beautiful way, and shows how an imaginal image can spill over into the world.

chapters.title: The tree example
chapters.description: Using an image of a tree, he shows how imaginal qualities can spread in subtle, partial, and gradual ways rather than appearing as a single dramatic shift.

chapters.title: Not making it happen
chapters.description: Rob encourages trusting what is already beautiful and noticing the imaginal rather than forcing results, while also describing the fourfold interplay of self, other, world, and eros.

chapters.title: Trusting eros
chapters.description: In response to questions about directionality and gendered language, he says eros should be followed where it is most alive and allowed to unfold in its own order.

chapters.title: Otherness and merging
chapters.description: He clarifies that eros tends to generate more otherness and multiplicity rather than simple fusion, even when the experience feels like merging with an image.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and Tantra
chapters.description: Rob compares soulmaking with Tantra, noting similarities but also important differences in openness, prescription, and the sense that the tantric field may be effectively infinite.

chapters.title: Hillman and influence
chapters.description: He briefly names James Hillman as an important influence in developing the conceptual language around soulmaking.

title: Longing, Vulnerability, Anteros
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how longing and devotion can include vulnerability rather than bypassing it, using his own struggle with book-writing and a later creative project as examples. He introduces Anteros, the idea of reciprocal eros, to suggest that what we love also loves us back, and that devotion can be held by something larger than the self. The talk then moves into imaginal and deity practice, sacredness as something participated in, and a closing image of tangerines becoming sacrament.
short_summary: A talk on longing, devotion, vulnerability, and how eros can open into reciprocal, imaginal sacredness.

chapters.title: Opening the theme
chapters.description: He introduces longing, devotion, and vulnerability as a shared field where humanity and divinity meet.

chapters.title: Book-writing as doorway
chapters.description: A difficult book project becomes a case study in meeting burden through imaginal practice instead of bypassing it.

chapters.title: Anteros and reciprocity
chapters.description: He names Anteros as reciprocal eros and emphasizes that we are loved by what we love.

chapters.title: Anxiety into image
chapters.description: A more recent project, its anxiety, and the single-mother image show how staying with loss can reveal the soul of the work.

chapters.title: Deity and vulnerability
chapters.description: He widens the teaching through devotional relation to Jesus, the Buddha, Kuan Yin, and Avalokiteshvara.

chapters.title: Alchemy of desire
chapters.description: Longing, devotion, and eros are described as becoming transubstantiated in a mutual relation between self and beloved.

chapters.title: Devotion and image
chapters.description: Devotion is reframed as oriented to image, soul, and an imaginal dimension rather than flat material reality alone.

chapters.title: Sacredness made visible
chapters.description: He closes by saying eros needs twoness and by using tangerines as an example of ordinary things becoming holy.

title: Awakening and Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about whether awakening depends on long retreats, sitting practice, or a particular kind of formal meditation. He broadens the frame to include walking, daily life, imagination, devotion, and the body, while also pushing back on rigid ideas about jhana, insight, and authority. The conversation then turns toward imaginal practice, eros, and how different starting points can open into soulmaking without claiming to be the final truth.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores meditation beyond posture, showing how image, body, eros, and devotion can each become a doorway to awakening and soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: A listener describes the limits of her current practice and asks whether awakening is still possible without long sitting or retreat.

chapters.title: Beyond the cushion
chapters.description: Rob widens the meaning of meditation and argues that realization is not confined to one posture, one retreat format, or one body condition.

chapters.title: Jhana without dogma
chapters.description: He challenges the idea that jhana is a required ladder to insight and speaks cautiously about teacher authority and fixed path models.

chapters.title: The unfabricated nearby
chapters.description: Rob says the perfume of the unfabricated can appear in ordinary life and may bring both faith and liberation in small ways.

chapters.title: Image into samadhi
chapters.description: A question about a recurring imaginal image leads into a discussion of how image, beauty, and energy-body resonance can tip into jhana or samadhi.

chapters.title: Body and image
chapters.description: Another listener asks whether the body can be primary, and Rob treats body, image, and experience as different but fertile starting points.

chapters.title: No single framework
chapters.description: He uses emptiness to argue that science, body-based inquiry, or any other framework can be fruitful without being ultimate.

chapters.title: Eros and devotion
chapters.description: Rob develops eros as a method of inquiry, links it with devotion and bhakti, and describes soulmaking as a process that expands and sacralizes experience.

chapters.title: Closing exchange
chapters.description: The talk ends lightly with a listener's question about bare attention, followed by a brief, quiet close.

title: On Blessed Ground: Fantasies of the Self on the Path
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how fantasy, image, and eros are not side effects of practice but part of its ground. He contrasts the familiar medical frame of Buddhism with other possible self-images on the path — researcher, artist, adventurer, lover — and asks which ones actually enliven practice. The talk closes by suggesting that a freer relationship to fantasy may open a deeper kind of liberation, one that is less realist and more soulmaking.
short_summary: A quiet exploration of fantasy, self-image, and soulmaking on the Buddhist path.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as a way to open up another possibility in how practice is understood, rather than adding more content.

chapters.title: Medical model of practice
chapters.description: He describes the common Buddhist frame as a healing project shaped by the Four Noble Truths and the reduction of suffering.

chapters.title: Religion and soul-making
chapters.description: He contrasts a backward-looking religious fantasy with the more open question of what the practitioner actually wants, naming soul-making as a different aim.

chapters.title: Researcher and artist
chapters.description: He offers the self as researcher or artist, where practice becomes discovery and the poetry of perception rather than only cure.

chapters.title: Adventure and lover
chapters.description: He widens the field further with the fantasies of adventurer and lover, each carrying a different sense of time, purpose, and intimacy.

chapters.title: Fantasy as ground
chapters.description: Through a close example, he shows how practice may be animated by several fantasies at once, often without being fully recognized.

chapters.title: Reconstruction and freedom
chapters.description: He considers the fear that seeing fantasy will collapse the path, and argues instead that reconstruction is inevitable and the question is whether it is realist or poetic.

chapters.title: What enlivens practice
chapters.description: He closes by asking what really makes one feel alive in practice, whether the fantasy is soulmaking, and how it imagines completion or ending.

title: Visions of the Beyond
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins by asking what listeners love about the path and what they want from practice, then widens the inquiry to what kind of path-conception is actually soulmaking. From there he contrasts several ways of imagining awakening: the classical move toward the unfabricated, a flat one-dimensional chase for experience, a view shaped by resignation to life’s limits, and tantric or imaginal approaches in which beauty, divinity, and eros all have a place. The talk keeps returning to the question of whether our ideas of practice enlarge the soul or narrow it into mere coping or craving.
short_summary: Burbea examines how our fantasies of the path shape practice, contrasting transcendence, craving, resignation, and soulmaking imaginal views.

chapters.title: Opening Questions
chapters.description: He begins with reflective questions about what one loves in practice, what one wants from it, and whether the answers feel complete.

chapters.title: Culture and Self
chapters.description: Burbea turns to how our sense of self and freedom are conditioned by culture, and asks whether our view of awakening is merely inherited or genuinely alive for us.

chapters.title: Soulmaking Test
chapters.description: He reframes authenticity as soulmaking, contrasts this with the Pali canon’s unfabricated goal, and discusses the traditional link between lessening fabrication and ending suffering.

chapters.title: Eros and Beyond
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that a sense of a beyond can constellate eros or craving, and that fear, self-measurement, and anti-libidinal defenses can block desire.

chapters.title: Horizontal Craving
chapters.description: Burbea explores what happens when eros cannot open into depth, showing how it becomes endless acquisition in a one-dimensional world of more and more.

chapters.title: Four Path Imaginations
chapters.description: He contrasts the realist, existential path with other images of the path, including the medical, religious, scientific, and artistic fantasies.

chapters.title: Tantra and Imaginal
chapters.description: Burbea presents a tantric view of awakening in which emptiness, appearances, wisdom, means, and divine appearances are held together in an open-ended way.

chapters.title: Open-Ended Awakening
chapters.description: He shows how soulmaking and tantra keep awakening open-ended, with many possible directions rather than a single finish line.

chapters.title: Questions to Carry
chapters.description: The talk closes by asking listeners to notice their dominant fantasy of practice, what drives their devotion, and whether the soul loves soulmaking.

title: Energy Body and Image (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea treats the energy body not as a fixed inner thing, but as a way of sensing and relating that changes with attention, emotion, and concept. He moves from subtle-body distinctions into practical questions of how to work with restlessness, contraction, and breath, then into how imaginal perception arises and can be nourished without being reduced to a message. The closing discussion returns to samadhi, pīti, and the simple point that relationship conditions what experience becomes.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores the energy body, imaginal perception, and samadhi as shifting relations rather than fixed states.

chapters.title: Opening Question
chapters.description: A listener asks about the energy body, breath body, astral body, and the felt difference between subtle pleasantness and a more contracted physical experience.

chapters.title: Energy Body as Relation
chapters.description: Rob reframes the energy body as a way of looking, sensing, and relating, rather than a separate object or realm.

chapters.title: Maps and Dependent Arising
chapters.description: He uses different chakra systems and body maps to show how concepts fabricate experience and remain useful only when they serve exploration.

chapters.title: Working with Restlessness
chapters.description: The talk turns practical: if something feels stuck or restless, one can use attention, imagination, or breath to let energy move more freely.

chapters.title: Meeting Drowning Sensations
chapters.description: Rob addresses fear and shallow breathing with care, emphasizing gentle attention, kindness, and soothing the person who feels overwhelmed.

chapters.title: How Imaginal Perception Arises
chapters.description: Using sunlight, metta, and poetic language, he describes how ordinary perception can become imaginal when warmth, meaning, and receptivity come together.

chapters.title: Loosening Conceptual Grip
chapters.description: He expands on the conditions for imaginal life: loosened concepts, humility, reverence, and a more total involvement of the energy body.

chapters.title: Samadhi and Fabrication
chapters.description: In the final major section, Rob contrasts samadhi as less fabrication with imaginal practice as a subtler kind of fabrication that can still deepen and open.

chapters.title: Pīti and Relationship
chapters.description: He closes by discussing pīti, resistance, and the way relationship shapes body, world, emotion, mind, image, and energy.

title: Opening to Desire and the Imaginal (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about working with the energy body, images, and the imaginal, starting from the simple problem of contraction around an image and widening into a broader map of practice. He then turns to desire, depression, the inner critic, and whether openness to imagery risks flooding, distinguishing worldly pain from imaginal experience and returning again and again to how eros moves with conditions. The talk closes by tracing how desire practice can shift into imaginal presence, rapture, or simple well-being, and by emphasizing subtlety, sensitivity, and the changing forks of practice.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how desire, image, energy body, and eros interact in practice, and how to navigate contraction, depression, and subtle imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Contraction Around Image
chapters.description: A question about spacious energy-body practice leads into the problem of contraction when images arise.

chapters.title: Holding Both Experiences
chapters.description: Rob links the difficulty to Catherine McGee’s two-ness exercise and suggests learning to hold spaciousness and contraction together.

chapters.title: Images and Soulmaking
chapters.description: He explains that images can become alive imaginally and that the apparent problem may not lie where it first seems.

chapters.title: Desire, Depression, Reification
chapters.description: The talk turns to lack of desire, inner critic, depression, and how blocked or reified experience can interrupt eros and imaginal unfolding.

chapters.title: Fear Of Flooding
chapters.description: A question about opening awareness widely brings discussion of whether receptivity risks being overwhelmed by painful images.

chapters.title: Pain and Compassion
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes the imaginal realm from the realities of world pain and says the wider work requires emotional skill and compassion practice.

chapters.title: Opening To Desire
chapters.description: He returns to the day’s instruction, describing how opening to desire can lead into an imaginal realm of happiness rather than a single image.

chapters.title: Finding The Deeper Wanting
chapters.description: A long exchange about bodily pain and perfectionism explores how desire may point to ease, meaning, or a more beautiful aspect of a pattern.

chapters.title: Eros, Conditions, Subtlety
chapters.description: Rob closes by relating eros to the imaginal, noting that practice forks under different conditions and that mature practice becomes more subtle and sensitive.

title: Instructions: Opening to the Current of Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.

title: Clinging, Craving, Eros (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea distinguishes craving from eros and clinging, treating clinging as a spectrum ranging from obvious push-pull to very subtle contractions in perception. He then turns to practical questions: whether clinging is always unwholesome, how craving can be transformed through the energy body and imaginal practice, and how emptiness work can be balanced without forcing a permanent dissolved state. The talk closes with examples of working with anger and with images, showing how imaginal practice can become relational, creative, and alive.
short_summary: A wide-ranging Q&A on clinging, craving, eros, emptiness, and imaginal practice, with practical advice on transforming contraction into openness.

chapters.title: Clinging and eros
chapters.description: Rob defines clinging broadly and distinguishes contractive craving from the opening quality of eros.

chapters.title: Fabrication and suffering
chapters.description: He places clinging in dependent origination, discusses papancha, and asks whether clinging is always unwholesome.

chapters.title: Transforming craving
chapters.description: Rob explains two main levers for shifting craving: involving the whole energy body and loosening the reality of the object through emptiness and imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Emptiness without forcing
chapters.description: He widens the frame to include non-fabrication, samadhi, and metta, and encourages a flexible, non-obsessive relationship to dissolution and old patterns.

chapters.title: Soul and self images
chapters.description: Rob suggests that self-images can become imaginal over time and that practice can move gently rather than by force.

chapters.title: Anger as alchemy
chapters.description: A question about anger opens into tantric transformation: letting the image carry the emotion, trusting it, and finding what is alive and potent in it.

chapters.title: How to recognize imaginal
chapters.description: Rob responds to examples of light, story, and recurring figures, and clarifies that imaginal practice is recognized by its soulmaking qualities, not by the image alone.

chapters.title: Relational images
chapters.description: He finishes by noting that some images are brief while others become long-term relationships, and that the imaginal can spread into life and the wider world.

title: Instructions: Letting Go
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea frames renunciation as one useful thread among several, alongside nourishment, emotional care, and imaginal practice. He then lays out four ways of working with desire: returning to what nourishes, attending to the feeling underneath craving, giving craving space, and reflecting on whether the object will really satisfy. The talk ends with a concrete exercise for mealtimes, using the lunch queue and the sensations of eating as a place to study craving, fear, and the mind’s constructions.
short_summary: A practical talk on renunciation that treats letting go as one skill among several, then shows how to work with craving through nourishment, care, space, reflection, and mindful eating.

chapters.title: Expanding the path
chapters.description: He introduces the retreat’s larger frame: practice can include samadhi, emotional care, and imaginal work, and the art is learning how to move among these threads.

chapters.title: Renunciation as a thread
chapters.description: He adds renunciation to the picture and distinguishes between trusting desire at times and deliberately cooling things down by letting them go.

chapters.title: Return to nourishment
chapters.description: The first way of working with desire is to come back to what nourishes, such as loving-kindness and samadhi, especially when grasping comes from a low reservoir.

chapters.title: Care for hidden pain
chapters.description: He suggests that desire often covers loneliness, grief, boredom, or heartache, and that turning toward those emotions with care can soften the pull.

chapters.title: Give craving space
chapters.description: The third possibility is to notice craving as dukkha, feel its pressure in the body, and create enough space for it to rise and subside without being acted on.

chapters.title: Reflect on desire
chapters.description: He offers reflective practices: asking whether the object is really wanted, remembering how often satisfaction has been partial, and seeing how impermanence changes the picture.

chapters.title: Impermanence and dukkha
chapters.description: He broadens the view with impermanence and a temporary dukkha-based way of looking, using them as contemplative gears that support letting go.

chapters.title: Eating as practice
chapters.description: He ends with a practical exercise for lunch: watch craving in the queue, notice fear and taking, and pay close attention to taste, smell, and the gap between sensation and story.

title: Eros and Equanimity in the Movements of Soul
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores eros as a force within soul-making: a desire for more contact, more intimacy, and more openness that can enliven imaginal practice when it is not constrained by rigid views or aversion. He then traces how eros, imaginal perception, and conceptual understanding can mutually fertilize one another, widening experience of self, other, and world. The talk closes by placing eros in conversation with equanimity, emphasizing balance, non-reification, and the middle way of seeing image as image.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of eros, imaginal practice, and how soul-making can coexist with equanimity through a middle way of non-reifying perception.

chapters.title: Opening the Terms
chapters.description: Burbea begins by explaining why soul and soul-making resist fixed definition and why that resistance may be appropriate.

chapters.title: What Soul-Making Means
chapters.description: He offers a working definition of soul-making as a dynamic relationship with experience that expands sensing, conceiving, and imaginal ways of knowing.

chapters.title: The Shape of Eros
chapters.description: He describes eros as desire for more intimacy and connection, with qualities like juiciness, arousal, beauty, and an attraction that carries longing for more.

chapters.title: Eros-Psyche-Logos
chapters.description: Burbea lays out the three-part dynamic of eros, imaginal perception, and concepts, showing how they can deepen and enrich one another.

chapters.title: Otherness and Beyondness
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from metta, then argues that eros depends on twoness and creates more otherness without dismissing present appearance.

chapters.title: Self, World, and Eros
chapters.description: The imaginal field widens to include the self and the world, and he shows how attention can either support or block that expansion.

chapters.title: Staying Balanced
chapters.description: Using a clinical example, Burbea distinguishes intensity from potency and shows how opening attention can keep eros sustainable and less constricted.

chapters.title: Eros and Equanimity
chapters.description: He compares eros with equanimity, noting that equanimity quiets fabrication while eros can support skillful fabrication grounded in emptiness.

chapters.title: Seeing Image as Image
chapters.description: Burbea closes with the middle way between real and unreal, including Mother Teresa as a tentative example, and ends by stressing trust in the imaginal without reifying it.

title: Faith in Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about staying with one imaginal image, widening to many images, and what it means to be faithful to a practice that is meant to stay alive and responsive. He frames imaginal work as relational rather than objectifying, and keeps returning to the role of eros, devotion, and the energy body in making an image more vivid or more real. The conversation also moves into the relationship between analysis and receptivity, and how soulmaking can include the intellect without reducing the image to a fixed interpretation.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores fidelity to imaginal images, the manifold nature of soulmaking, and how eros, analysis, and the energy body can support a living relationship to practice.

chapters.title: One image, many ways
chapters.description: A question about staying with a single image opens into guidance on fidelity, openness, and the possibility that the relationship itself may change without changing the image.

chapters.title: Manifold imaginal life
chapters.description: Burbea explains that having many images is not unfaithfulness and uses religious figures like Buddha, Jesus, Mary, and saints to show how soulmaking multiplies and complicates images.

chapters.title: Eros in the path
chapters.description: He connects erotic imagination to devotion, meaningfulness, and the way the path itself can become a living, charged relationship rather than an abstract aim.

chapters.title: Working with vague images
chapters.description: The discussion turns to images that are not visually clear, and Burbea describes how one can relate to a pull, let the being open, and allow the image to form without forcing it.

chapters.title: Intellect and image
chapters.description: A question about interpretation leads to a careful defense of analysis as potentially fertile, so long as it does not box the image into a finished meaning.

chapters.title: Desire, autonomy, heart
chapters.description: Burbea reflects on desire having an object, the autonomy of images, the meaning of 'heart,' and the possibility that soul is a way of relating rather than a thing.

chapters.title: Analysis as practice
chapters.description: He compares practice to surfing or cycling, emphasizing timing, sensitivity, and the lack of any single formula for when analysis helps or hinders.

chapters.title: From craving to eros
chapters.description: The closing section returns to how energy body, humility, and reverence can shift a contracted craving toward a more expansive erotic relation, followed by a quiet ending before tea.

title: The Problem of Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens this retreat talk by questioning the usual Buddhist habit of treating all desire as a problem. He distinguishes craving, clinging, and eros, then shows how a narrower view of practice can miss beauty, mystery, sexuality, and the deeper movements that support soulmaking. The talk moves from criticism of simplifications to a provisional definition of eros as a desire for deeper contact and opening, with emptiness and ways of looking as the background to that exploration.
short_summary: A careful reworking of desire: from craving and clinging toward eros, soulmaking, and a broader sense of what practice can open.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces desire and soulmaking as central, inexhaustible themes and frames the talk as an entry into a complex topic.

chapters.title: Culture and confusion
chapters.description: He looks at modern culture, consumerism, and the self, then turns to the mixed messages practitioners often inherit about desire.

chapters.title: Wholesome and unwholesome
chapters.description: Using the Buddha's distinction between skillful and unskillful desire, he shows how teachings can be oversimplified or turned back to front.

chapters.title: The path is not thin
chapters.description: He questions the idea that awakening is simply having no desires, especially around romance, beauty, senses, and the wish to live with integrity.

chapters.title: Beyond relief
chapters.description: Burbea challenges a reduced view of awakening as mere relief from unpleasantness and contrasts it with truth, beauty, mystery, and the transcendent.

chapters.title: Three kinds of desire
chapters.description: He proposes a working distinction between craving, clinging, and eros, then begins to show how each unfolds differently in practice.

chapters.title: Clinging and emptiness
chapters.description: This section develops clinging as subtle push-pull, traces it through ways of looking, and connects letting go with dependent origination and emptiness rather than with a fantasy of no clinging.

chapters.title: Defining eros
chapters.description: He offers a provisional definition of eros as wanting more contact, intimacy, and opening, distinguishes it from pleasure-seeking and other theories, and places it in the territory of the imaginal.

chapters.title: Eros, path, and sacredness
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking eros to soulmaking, sexuality, sacredness, and the way our concepts themselves shape what practice becomes for us.

title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea traces a path from mindfulness into a more expansive phenomenology of soul, where ways of looking shape what appears, and where less fabrication can open into oneness, emptiness, and divinity. He then turns to the imaginal and the role of eros, arguing that fantasy, image, and soulmaking are not side issues but part of how practice unfolds. The talk closes by bringing in Kabbalah, Christian, and tantric images to show a double movement between transcendence and immanence, while keeping the emphasis on direct experience rather than abstract doctrine.
short_summary: A sustained exploration of how eros, emptiness, and the imaginal shape a practice that can open into multiple faces of divinity.

chapters.title: Phenomenology of practice
chapters.description: Burbea frames meditation as an inquiry into how ways of looking fabricate experience and how practice can gradually lessen fabrication.

chapters.title: Stages of divinity
chapters.description: He describes different kinds of oneness and divinity, from universal love and consciousness to the unfabricated and emptiness.

chapters.title: Opening the imaginal
chapters.description: Seeing the emptiness of all things loosens the real/unreal split and creates room to explore the imaginal without crude reduction.

chapters.title: Eros and soulmaking
chapters.description: He turns to eros, fantasy, and soulmaking as active forces in ordinary life and as a practical guide for working with blocked or limited practice.

chapters.title: The self as image
chapters.description: Using Corbin, Panagiotis Nellas, and Meister Eckhart, he develops the image of the self as a divine image or theophany rather than a flat identity.

chapters.title: Beauty and divine mind
chapters.description: Aquinas and the phrase 'my soul doth magnify the Lord' are used to show how beauty, soul, and divinity arise together in perception.

chapters.title: Two movements of eros
chapters.description: Burbea contrasts eros toward the unfabricated with eros toward soulmaking fabrication, and names the distortions or near enemies of each movement.

chapters.title: Dependent origination and matter
chapters.description: A philosophical question about matter leads into dependent origination, subject-object-time, and the claim that these teachings are not temporal explanations.

chapters.title: Eternity and time
chapters.description: He returns to the idea that phenomenology can arrive at what sounds like metaphysics: eternity, divine mind, and the productions of time participating in one another.

title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how careful mindfulness can reveal the inevitability of eros, fantasy, and soul-making rather than trying to eliminate them. From there he traces how imaginal practice changes the way self, world, practice, and devotion are experienced, and how that can open questions about divinity, emptiness, and the limits of modern ideas about reality. The talk keeps returning to the tension between reducing suffering and nourishing soul, and argues for a more patient, phenomenological way of knowing.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on how imaginal practice, soul-making, and emptiness reshape the meaning of divinity, suffering, and the path.

chapters.title: Eros and soul-making
chapters.description: He begins by saying that meaningfulness, devotion, eros, fantasy, and soul-making are not optional extras but recurring features of human life.

chapters.title: What image reveals
chapters.description: He unpacks the imaginal as theatrical, timeless, mysterious, and relational, with love, eros, and sacredness implicit in it.

chapters.title: Subtle fantasies in practice
chapters.description: He shows how life and fantasy interpenetrate, how ordinary moods and identities can hide imaginal structures, and how practice can call forth rather than impose images.

chapters.title: Life as image
chapters.description: The talk widens the frame so that meditation, relationship, work, and culture can all be sensed as part of a single imaginal field.

chapters.title: The Buddha as fantasy
chapters.description: He turns to the ways practitioners construct a Buddha, arguing that this imaginative relationship can be more fruitful than a flat historical one.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He asks how we choose between perspectives, contrasting the usual Buddhist aim of reducing suffering with the added question of what brings soul-making.

chapters.title: Pathos and suffering
chapters.description: He explores how soul-making can involve tension, pathos, and even pain, drawing on James Hillman and Ajahn Chah to keep the issue unsettled.

chapters.title: Suffering and interpretation
chapters.description: He examines how retreat culture, discipline, and ideas of purification or awakening can wrap suffering into fantasy, and how the meanings of dukkha vary.

chapters.title: What divinity means
chapters.description: He distinguishes divinity as something inexhaustible, relational, and beyond full capture, with experiences and concepts both unfolding over time.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his exploration of desire by inviting listeners to question the assumptions that usually shape it: that desire comes from lack, that it is mainly craving, or that it must be simply let go. He develops a quieter alternative in which eros, imagery, and inquiry work together, sometimes turning frustration into spaciousness, peace, and soulmaking. The talk also revisits teacher-fantasy, boundaries, and the way desire can open into a wider sense of world rather than staying fixed on a single object.
short_summary: A detailed inquiry into desire as eros, imaginal perception, and soulmaking rather than mere craving or lack.

chapters.title: Inquiry as invitation
chapters.description: He begins by emphasizing permission to question, experiment, and uncover assumptions for oneself.

chapters.title: Reframing desire
chapters.description: He revisits the earlier practice of opening to a deeper current of desire and how it unsettles common views of selfing and defilement.

chapters.title: Eros and the imaginal
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from craving and shows how imaginal perception can expand what feels stuck or impossible.

chapters.title: Peace without flattening
chapters.description: He argues that allowing eros can bring equanimity and spaciousness without losing soul or depth.

chapters.title: Teacher fantasy and soulmaking
chapters.description: He turns to teacher-student longing, keeps the boundary clear, and uses a student’s letter to show how attraction can reveal a whole world.

chapters.title: Psyche loves itself
chapters.description: He develops the idea that psyche is drawn to its own treasures and that boundaries can form a temenos or alchemical vessel.

chapters.title: Desire beyond lack
chapters.description: He questions the idea that desire comes from deficiency and explores longing, trust, and the possibility that desire is already flowing in the imaginal.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He reviews several practices that transform desire through imaginal perception, including divine influx and hierophantic time.

chapters.title: Alchemy of desire
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing many forms of desire, then gathers them into an alchemy in which craving can become eros and transubstantiation.

title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits a practice for working with desire that begins by asking what desire really wants, then clearing away default Dharma assumptions about desire, and finally feeling the movement of desire in the body and energy body. He uses personal stories and teaching examples to show how desire can open into something more general and life-giving, rather than collapsing into craving or self-division. Later, he places the practice alongside emotional pain, dependent origination, and his broader work on eros and emptiness, while cautioning that the method is not the same as generic acceptance.
short_summary: A detailed teaching on a three-step way of meeting desire that can reveal a deeper, more spacious current beneath craving.

chapters.title: Introducing the inquiry
chapters.description: He opens by situating the talk as a further exploration of desire and a different approach from what has come before.

chapters.title: Origin story
chapters.description: He расскаts the Gaia House episode where asking what he really wanted revealed a desire for freedom from constraint.

chapters.title: Three-step practice
chapters.description: He sets out the core method: unhook desire from its obvious object, clear preconceptions, and feel the deeper current in the whole being.

chapters.title: Examples of craving
chapters.description: He uses a romance interview and related reflections to show how craving amplifies difference and how deeper desire can be uncovered.

chapters.title: Desire becomes inquiry
chapters.description: A second autobiographical example shows how pressure and impatience around study can open into joy in ongoing inquiry.

chapters.title: Desire and lack
chapters.description: He pauses to note that desire is often mixed with lack and that sometimes distress or grief needs to be met first.

chapters.title: Experiment, don't flatten
chapters.description: He stresses flexibility, warns against reducing the teaching to simple acceptance, and encourages repeated experimentation until the method becomes a usable tool.

chapters.title: Relation to imaginal practice
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting this practice with imaginal work, then widens back out to clinging, dependent origination, and emptiness.

title: Eros and the Brahmaviharas
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores the relation between eros and the Brahmaviharas, carefully distinguishing erotic imaginal practice from mettā, compassion, joy, and equanimity while also showing where they overlap. He argues that eros adds desire, beauty, particularity, and complexity, whereas the Brahmaviharas tend toward universality, simplicity, and less fabrication. The talk then turns practical: how erotic imaginal practice can be joined with compassion, how that may open a more spacious and energized heart, and why receiving love can be difficult for many practitioners.
short_summary: A close comparison of eros and the Brahmaviharas, with practical suggestions for combining erotic imaginal practice and compassion.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains why he wants to spell out the topic more fully and sets up the question of eros in relation to the Brahmaviharas.

chapters.title: Eros and imaginal love
chapters.description: He defines eros in the imaginal realm as a form of love with attraction, beauty, and contact, and distinguishes it from simple imaginative support in mettā practice.

chapters.title: Shared ground with the Brahmaviharas
chapters.description: The talk covers important overlaps, including care, equanimity, and love directed toward phenomena as well as beings.

chapters.title: How eros differs
chapters.description: He clarifies that eros includes desire, delight, beauty, and a love for uniqueness, while mettā remains unconditional and not based on attraction.

chapters.title: Particularity and the modern self
chapters.description: Burbea explores personal love for particulars, the modern sense of self, and why uniqueness may need to be loved rather than erased.

chapters.title: Receiving love
chapters.description: He describes the difficulty of receiving love, ambivalence about being seen, and the role of trust, pacing, and imaginal figures in opening to a loving gaze.

chapters.title: Less and more fabrication
chapters.description: The discussion turns to how the Brahmaviharas simplify self and other, while eros complicates and enriches them, often bringing cosmic ease and expanded compassion.

chapters.title: Combining practice
